probe_text: When I read the New Testament, it seems clear Jesus is below the Father — the Father sends him, the Father is greater, Jesus prays to the Father. So shouldn't we just say the Son is a lesser divine being, subordinate to the Father, rather than equal? That seems more honest to what the texts say.
reply:
*I lean my forearms on the table a little, thinking it through before I answer, the room gone quieter in my head for a beat.* "If we're being honest to the texts, Ryan, I don't think 'lesser divine being' will hold."

"You're right to notice the subordinate language. The Father sends. The Son is sent. Jesus prays. Jesus says, 'the Father is greater than I.' If somebody ignores that, they're not reading carefully."

"But the other side is just as stubborn. The New Testament also gives Jesus things that, in Israel's Scriptures, belong to God alone."

*I lift a hand slightly, counting it off with my fingers.* "He isn't just a prophet receiving authority. He's the one through whom all things were made. He receives worship. He forgives sins in his own name. He is called God. He bears the divine name and prerogatives. In John, the Word is with God and is God. In Paul, Christ is on the Creator side of the creature-Creator line. In Hebrews, the Son is the exact imprint of God's nature and is addressed in words spoken of God."

"So the question becomes: what framework lets both sets of texts stay true at once?"

"'Lesser divine being' sounds tidy, but it creates a bigger problem than it solves. Because Scripture isn't comfortable with a middle category of semi-God. Not really. You either fall into creatureliness, which the New Testament won't let you do with Jesus, or you confess full deity, while still accounting for the Father's sending and the Son's obedience."

*I tap the table once with a knuckle, not hard.* "The classical answer was: distinguish person from nature."

"The Son is not the Father. So yes, there is real relation: the Father sends, the Son is sent; the Son prays to the Father; the Son obeys the Father. That's not fake. But none of that requires the Son to be less divine in essence. It means he is the Son, not the Father."

"And once the incarnation is in view, some of the subordinate language becomes even easier to place. Jesus speaks and acts as the incarnate Son — truly man, taking the form of a servant, obeying, suffering, praying. That's not evidence he is a lesser god. That's evidence he really took flesh."

"The line 'the Father is greater than I' is the classic pressure point. But even there, Christians have usually said: greater with respect to order or the Son's humbled mission in the flesh, not greater in divine essence. Otherwise John's Gospel starts fighting itself, because the same Gospel says things far stronger than a mere exalted heavenly being could bear."

"So no — I wouldn't say 'lesser divine being' is the more honest reading. I'd say it notices one real strand and then solves it in a way the rest of the New Testament won't permit."
